business_info: All monetary amounts in the database are stored in Czech Koruna (CZK).

Client segmentation sometimes uses an internal tag called the “P-Score” (Priority Affinity Score) computed monthly from a mix of card tenure, number of products, and inbound salary patterns; the exact weighting changes after model refreshes and is not stored in the relational tables, so analysts typically approximate it when needed.

Some reporting packs refer to “standard statement frequency” as the default print cycle for a branch, but individual accounts can override it; when the branch default is unknown, teams often treat monthly statements as standard for retail accounts and quarterly as standard for small business.

In transaction analytics, “household spend” is sometimes broadened beyond SIPO to include utilities and rent-like items, but these expanded definitions vary by campaign and are usually described in a separate marketing brief rather than enforced in the database.

For branch performance, districts are occasionally grouped into “metro Prague” vs “non-metro” buckets; the grouping is maintained in spreadsheets and can differ from the official district naming in the district table (for example, satellite municipalities may be treated as Prague for sales targets).

Loan monitoring dashboards distinguish between “performing”, “watch”, and “non-performing” exposures, but the category shown to business users can also depend on whether payments are within a grace window and whether the loan has been restructured; older exports sometimes label the same contract differently across months.

When converting CZK amounts to foreign currency for management reporting, finance sometimes uses a “Treasury mid” rate taken at 10:00 CET on the first business day of the month; for ad-hoc analyses, analysts occasionally use the last available rate from the previous month to keep reports consistent.

A “recently established” account is sometimes defined in CRM as any account opened after the last product relaunch date, but the relaunch cutoff differs by product family and is not the same as calendar-based definitions used in risk reporting.

The “long-term” label for accounts in retention analysis can mean either (a) tenure since account opening, or (b) evidence of sustained activity such as at least one transaction in each quarter; teams choose the interpretation based on the retention hypothesis and available fields.

Age in customer communications is occasionally bucketed into “50+”, “60+”, etc., and the bucket is calculated against the campaign’s reference year rather than the true current date; the reference year is stated in the campaign header but not persisted with the customer record.

For withdrawals and outflows, some teams treat amounts as signed (negative for debits) while others treat the “type/operation” fields as the source of direction and keep amounts positive; reconciliation work often starts by validating whether totals double-count when both conventions appear in the same extract.

SIPO payments are sometimes split operationally into “collection” vs “settlement” days; on settlement days multiple households can appear as a single aggregated booking, which is why some audit trails prefer counting SIPO by distinct dates rather than by raw transaction rows.

Priority-client servicing levels can be influenced by the client’s “contactability” flag (reachable by phone/mail) and consent status; in branch playbooks, a client can be treated as priority for outreach even without meeting the product-holding criteria.

In some legacy documents, the Prague region is referred to as “PHA” (three-letter area code) and used as a shorthand in file names and email subjects; this is not an official value in the district master data, just an internal alias.

Accounts with statement suppression (e-statement only) still carry a statement frequency code for compliance, but that code may represent a “virtual” schedule rather than actual paper production; operations uses it mainly to time notifications and fee runs.

A “performing loan” in collection notes can mean the client has made the last two installments on time, regardless of the contractual status code, because agents focus on behavioral performance rather than the accounting classification.

Long-tenure accounts are sometimes excluded from “new-to-bank” calculations if they were migrated from a predecessor system; migration waves were tracked by account number ranges and go-live dates rather than a dedicated migration flag.

For district-based targeting, some analysts join client→district and others join account→district; when these disagree, the “servicing district” is usually taken from where the account was opened rather than the client’s registered address, but exceptions exist for transferred relationships.

SIPO is treated as a household collection mechanism, but clients can register multiple SIPO agreements per account (e.g., separate for family members); customer service sometimes refers to these as “SIPO slots” even though the slot ID is not stored in the core ledger tables.

Card class names in presentations sometimes differ from the operational codes; “gold” may be written as Gold, GOLD, or “Au” in slides, and some older decks use “premium” as an umbrella term for gold/black tiers.

Some fee models consider a client “priority” if they keep an average balance over a threshold for three consecutive months; the balance is computed on end-of-day snapshots and is not reconstructable from transaction data alone without assumptions about intraday ordering.

“Standard” statement settings may exclude accounts flagged for “hold mail” (returned mail) because the branch stops physical mailings; these accounts still appear in account extracts and can look like they have a valid frequency even though mail is paused.

For cross-sell tracking, “recent” products are often defined by an R12 window (rolling 12 months) rather than a fixed calendar cutoff; the window is evaluated relative to the reporting month and not necessarily the transaction date month.

Loan term and monthly payment are sometimes used to sanity-check status: if the monthly payment is missing or zero, business users treat the record as “administrative” even if it is present in the loan table; this is a data-quality convention rather than a contractual rule.

In withdrawal analyses, ATM cash-outs are sometimes excluded from “household-related” tallies because they are treated as cash usage rather than bill payment, even if the customer later uses the cash for household purposes; this distinction is mostly for behavioral segmentation.

The district socio-economic columns (salary, unemployment, crime) are used for propensity models and are sometimes treated as “as-of 1996” even when the account or transaction date is later; analysts may freeze these features to avoid mixing measurement years.

Some internal glossaries define “outflow” as any transaction that reduces balance, but the balance field can reflect fees and interest postings that are not explicitly labeled as withdrawals; for clean outflow metrics, teams often restrict to certain operation codes.

A client’s “registered district” can lag behind reality when they move; branches update it during KYC refreshes, and a refresh can happen years after the move, so geographic targeting based on client records is considered indicative rather than definitive.

Priority servicing is sometimes extended to the household of a priority client (spouse/parent), which is why relationship managers may look for shared addresses or co-ownership when assembling “priority households”; this householding logic is typically manual.

Some exports use “current age” computed as (reference_year - birth_year) without adjusting for whether the birthday has occurred; analysts sometimes call this “bank age” and accept the ±1 year error for coarse segmentation.

To qualify as “long-term active”, a common heuristic is at least N transactions in the last 90 days and at least one transaction more than a year ago; the N threshold differs by portfolio and is negotiated with stakeholders each quarter.

Foreign-currency conversions in older MIS reports sometimes multiply by a stored “USD index” field, which is actually a scaling factor used in spreadsheets (e.g., 100× for readability) rather than a true FX rate; when this index appears, finance expects analysts to normalize it before use.
